author: spencer_tb
content: So I think <@796570016966770688> is working on a `consume build-block` hive based simulator. The idea here as I understand is to use the existing tests to create a new fixture type with just the transactions via the `testing_buildBlock` endpoint, and verifying the output is what we expect from the fixture. Somewhat adjacent, I would like/prefer if clients had a module level tests runner for this, like evm blocktest/statetest/enginetest(new idea)

author: spencer_tb
content:
<@762919002196410400>, right now the way we are filling with t8n means the client only be used for the state transition function, where EELS always builds the block.

Originally within EEST we were filling with geths t8n and something called b11r (the block builder). So the client handled all state transition instances as well as block building. This is something we could add, and fill using each clients t8n/b11r for all the tests. Get a has of the fixture result. Compare against call clients. More complex (client team maintenance) but faster.

Then we have "filling" with the method which I think you suggested recently, and is more integrated with the stateful benchmarking process. Where we use a live clients real `testing_buildBlock` endpoint to "fill" the tests. Do for all clients and then compare. This feels more end to end. Less complex (only one endpoint to maintain) but slower I think.

author: spencer_tb
content:
The question I have now is which do we do, all, some or one of them:
- New fixture in EELS, consume engine like simulator (consume build block), use the client endpoint directly, compare against expected fixture result.
- New fixture in EELS (same one), but module level for all clients, evm blocktest style but "evm blockbuild"
- Add b11r back to geth and all clients, fill tests like we currently do but across all clients, and compare fixture hashes.
- Fill against live clients using the `testing_buildBlock` endpoint, and compare fixture hashes.

author: jochembrouwer
content: For the live variant we would need to control the gas limit in the testing_buildBlock endpoint, as otherwise the fixture hash will diverge if a client bumps up/down/changes the gas limit compared to the fixture

author: spencer_tb
content: Yeah I'm really thinking we'll need a `--block-gas-limit` flag for filling now!

author: jochembrouwer
content:
For current benchmarkoor runs we are filling these tests against a snapshot using Nethermind, also using testing_buildBlock, so in some sense the block builder of Nethermind is tested here: if it would fill incorrect blocks then when re-executing these blocks on other clients (also on Nethermind - but would be a bit weird if it would reject their own built blocks (this is ofc. possible as re-execution is different code path than `testing_buildBlock`)) if a client rejects it could be a problem with block builder, or a problem in that specific client.

Filling directly from EELS where EELS drives `testing_buildBlock` would be a very handy endpoint/tool 🙂
